47:
"Ashdod with her towns and her villages, Gaza with her towns and her villages, unto the river of Egypt, and the great sea, and the border thereof:" The witnesses note Ashdod and Gaza, Philistine cities, with the river of Egypt and the great sea as Judah's border. The witnesses note that Gill notes Gaza was another Philistine principality; these, with Gath and Ashkelon, were within Judah's lot. The witnesses note that the border ran to the river of Egypt and the great sea. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Ashdod and Gaza — philistine cities; the unto the river of Egypt — the southern bound; the the great sea — the western border; the left unsubdued — thorns in your sides (Judg. 2:3).

The witnesses agree the lot of Judah took in the great Philistine cities of Ashdod and Gaza, reaching "unto the river of Egypt, and the great sea": the whole southwestern coast was assigned to the tribe, the border drawn at the very stream of Egypt and the western waters; yet these strong cities of the Philistines were largely unsubdued, a thorn in Israel's side for ages; the wide and goodly bound, with so much left in enemy hands, witnesses both to the largeness of God's gift and the cost of incomplete obedience; it teaches that the people of God are to drive out wholly what he has given them to possess, lest the unconquered remain to vex them, "they shall be... thorns in your sides" (Judg. 2:3), Ashdod and Gaza within Judah's border yet unsubdued.

So the witnesses make the verse Ashdod and Gaza: The witnesses note the Ashdod and Gaza: Philistine cities. The witnesses note the unto the river of Egypt: the southern bound. The witnesses note the the great sea: the western border. The witnesses note the left unsubdued: thorns in your sides (Judg. 2:3). The expositors observe the Ashdod and Gaza: Philistine cities. The expositors observe the unto the river of Egypt: the southern bound. The expositors observe the the great sea: the western border. The expositors observe the left unsubdued: thorns in your sides (Judg. 2:3). The believer is taught to mark the Ashdod and Gaza, the unto the river of Egypt, the the great sea, the left unsubdued, and to rest in the sureness of his inheritance. The verse thus records that Ashdod and Gaza, with the river of Egypt and the great sea, marked Judah's border — the largeness of God's gift yet much left in enemy hands, the cost of incomplete obedience, the unconquered becoming thorns in their sides (Judg. 2:3).
48:
"And in the mountains, Shamir, and Jattir, and Socoh," The witnesses note the cities of Judah in the hill country, beginning with Shamir, Jattir, and Socoh. The witnesses note that Gill notes the hill country of Judea, in which were the following cities. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch note the mountain towns divided into groups, beginning with Shamir, Jattir, and Socoh. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the in the mountains — the hill country; the Shamir and Jattir — the highland cities; the Jattir a priests' city — set apart for God; the a full, varied portion — of his fulness have all received (John 1:16).

The witnesses agree the catalogue now ascends from the valley to "the mountains" — the hill country of Judah, beginning "Shamir, and Jattir, and Socoh": the heritage took in both the lowland plains and the highland hills, the whole varied country given to the tribe; Jattir was afterward a priestly city (Josh. 21:14), a town of the heights set apart for God's service; the inheritance of Judah, reaching from sea to hill, shows the fullness and variety of God's provision; it teaches that the LORD gives his people a complete and varied portion, the high places as well as the low, the believer's heritage rich and full, "of his fulness have all we received" (John 1:16), the hill country cities now named.

So the witnesses make the verse the hill country: The witnesses note the in the mountains: the hill country. The witnesses note the Shamir and Jattir: the highland cities. The witnesses note the Jattir a priests' city: set apart for God. The witnesses note the a full, varied portion: of his fulness have all received (John 1:16). The expositors observe the in the mountains: the hill country. The expositors observe the Shamir and Jattir: the highland cities. The expositors observe the Jattir a priests' city: set apart for God. The expositors observe the a full, varied portion: of his fulness have all received (John 1:16). The believer is taught to mark the in the mountains, the Shamir and Jattir, the Jattir a priests' city, the a full, varied portion, and to rest in the sureness of his inheritance. The verse thus records that the cities of the hill country are now named — the heritage taking in both lowland and highland, the fullness and variety of God's provision, of his fulness have all we received (John 1:16).
49:
"And Dannah, and Kirjathsannah, which is Debir," The witnesses note Dannah and Kirjath-sannah, which is Debir, among the hill cities of Judah. The witnesses note that Gill notes Dannah is not mentioned elsewhere. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch note Kirjath-sannah is Debir. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Dannah — little known; the Kirjath-sannah — which is Debir; the Othniel took it — winning Achsah; the faith remembered — god forgets not your labour of love (Heb. 6:10).

The witnesses agree the list names Debir, also called Kirjath-sannah and Kirjath-sepher ("city of the book"): this was the very town that Othniel took, winning Caleb's daughter Achsah, as told earlier in this chapter (Josh. 15:15-17); the city of valour and reward now stands in the roll of Judah's heritage; the place where faith and courage were crowned with blessing is numbered among the tribe's towns; it teaches that God records the scenes of his people's faithful deeds, the city of Othniel's valour kept in the heritage, the brave and believing remembered, "God is not unrighteous to forget your work and labour of love" (Heb. 6:10), Debir the city of the book among Judah's hill towns.

So the witnesses make the verse Debir the city of valour: The witnesses note the Dannah: little known. The witnesses note the Kirjath-sannah: which is Debir. The witnesses note the Othniel took it: winning Achsah. The witnesses note the faith remembered: God forgets not your labour of love (Heb. 6:10). The expositors observe the Dannah: little known. The expositors observe the Kirjath-sannah: which is Debir. The expositors observe the Othniel took it: winning Achsah. The expositors observe the faith remembered: God forgets not your labour of love (Heb. 6:10). The believer is taught to mark the Dannah, the Kirjath-sannah, the Othniel took it, the faith remembered, and to rest in the sureness of his inheritance. The verse thus records that Debir, the city Othniel took for Achsah, is named among Judah's hill towns — the scene of faithful valour kept in the heritage, the brave remembered, God not forgetting their labour of love (Heb. 6:10).
50:
"And Anab, and Eshtemoh, and Anim," The witnesses note Anab, Eshtemoh, and Anim — Eshtemoh afterward a priestly city. The witnesses note that Gill notes Anab, of which he speaks elsewhere. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch note Eshtemoa was ceded to the priests (Joshua 21:14; 1 Chr. 6:57). The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Anab — of the hill country; the Eshtemoh — a priestly city; the Anim — among the towns; the the ministry provided — they shall teach Jacob thy judgments (Deut. 33:10).

The witnesses agree the list names Eshtemoh, afterward "ceded to the priests" as a Levitical city: another of Judah's hill towns set apart for the ministers of God; the dispersing of priestly cities through the tribe placed the servants of the LORD among the people, that his worship and word might be near; the granting of these towns to the priests shows God's care to keep his service in the midst of his people; it teaches that the LORD provides for the maintenance of his ministry and the spread of his truth among the tribes, the priests set in cities throughout the land, "they shall teach Jacob thy judgments" (Deut. 33:10), Eshtemoh among the priests' cities of Judah.

So the witnesses make the verse Eshtemoh the priests' city: The witnesses note the Anab: of the hill country. The witnesses note the Eshtemoh: a priestly city. The witnesses note the Anim: among the towns. The witnesses note the the ministry provided: they shall teach Jacob thy judgments (Deut. 33:10). The expositors observe the Anab: of the hill country. The expositors observe the Eshtemoh: a priestly city. The expositors observe the Anim: among the towns. The expositors observe the the ministry provided: they shall teach Jacob thy judgments (Deut. 33:10). The believer is taught to mark the Anab, the Eshtemoh, the Anim, the the ministry provided, and to rest in the sureness of his inheritance. The verse thus records that Eshtemoh, later a priestly city, is named among Judah's hill towns — God's care to keep his service in the midst of his people, the priests dispersed to teach, they shall teach Jacob thy judgments (Deut. 33:10).
51:
"And Goshen, and Holon, and Giloh; eleven cities with their villages:" The witnesses note the close of this group — eleven cities, including Giloh. The witnesses note that Gill notes Goshen in the land of Canaan, of which he speaks elsewhere. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch note Goshen, Holon, and Giloh, eleven cities with their villages. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Goshen — and Holon; the Giloh — home of Ahithophel; the eleven cities — with their villages; the the betrayer among them — mine own friend lifted his heel (Ps. 41:9).

The witnesses agree the group closes with Giloh, the home of Ahithophel, David's counsellor who turned traitor (2 Sam. 15:12): the city of Judah's heritage was also the birthplace of one whose name became a byword for betrayed counsel; the mingling of the honoured and the shameful in the history of these towns reminds us that the inheritance held both faithful and faithless sons; it teaches that even among God's people there are those who betray, and that the heritage is no guarantee of the heart, "mine own familiar friend... hath lifted up his heel against me" (Ps. 41:9) — a word fulfilled in Ahithophel and at last in Judas; Giloh among the eleven cities of Judah.

So the witnesses make the verse Giloh of Ahithophel: The witnesses note the Goshen: and Holon. The witnesses note the Giloh: home of Ahithophel. The witnesses note the eleven cities: with their villages. The witnesses note the the betrayer among them: mine own friend lifted his heel (Ps. 41:9). The expositors observe the Goshen: and Holon. The expositors observe the Giloh: home of Ahithophel. The expositors observe the eleven cities: with their villages. The expositors observe the the betrayer among them: mine own friend lifted his heel (Ps. 41:9). The believer is taught to mark the Goshen, the Giloh, the eleven cities, the the betrayer among them, and to rest in the sureness of his inheritance. The verse thus records that Giloh, the home of Ahithophel the traitor, closes the group of eleven — the heritage holding both faithful and faithless sons, the betrayer foreshadowed, mine own familiar friend lifting up his heel against me (Ps. 41:9).
52:
"Arab, and Dumah, and Eshean," The witnesses note Arab, Dumah, and Eshean, beginning a second group of hill cities. The witnesses note that Gill notes Arab is the Ereb of Jerom, in his time a village in the south. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the second group of nine towns to the north of the former. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Arab — a southern village; the Dumah — silence or stillness; the Eshean — of the group; the rest in stillness — be still, and know that I am God (Ps. 46:10).

The witnesses agree a second group of hill towns is now named, "Arab, and Dumah, and Eshean": the name "Dumah" means "silence" or "stillness," a word elsewhere used of the silence of the grave and of solemn waiting; the quiet town among Judah's cities may remind the believer that there is a place for stillness before God; the ordering of the inheritance into ever-new groups shows the patient completeness of the record; it teaches that God gives his people both their busy and their quiet places, and that the soul finds its rest in stillness before him, "be still, and know that I am God" (Ps. 46:10), Dumah the city of silence among Judah's towns.

So the witnesses make the verse Dumah the city of silence: The witnesses note the Arab: a southern village. The witnesses note the Dumah: silence or stillness. The witnesses note the Eshean: of the group. The witnesses note the rest in stillness: be still, and know that I am God (Ps. 46:10). The expositors observe the Arab: a southern village. The expositors observe the Dumah: silence or stillness. The expositors observe the Eshean: of the group. The expositors observe the rest in stillness: be still, and know that I am God (Ps. 46:10). The believer is taught to mark the Arab, the Dumah, the Eshean, the rest in stillness, and to rest in the sureness of his inheritance. The verse thus records that a second group of hill towns is named, with Dumah, "silence" — God giving his people both busy and quiet places, the soul resting in stillness before him, be still, and know that I am God (Ps. 46:10).
53:
"And Janum, and Bethtappuah, and Aphekah," The witnesses note Janum, Beth-tappuah, and Aphekah among the hill cities. The witnesses note that Gill notes under "Janum" Jerom writes of a village called Janua, three miles from Legion. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set Janum, Beth-tappuah, and Aphekah. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Janum — a hill town; the Beth-tappuah — the house of the apple; the Aphekah — among the cities; the a fruitful land — a land wherein thou shalt eat bread without scarceness (Deut. 8:8-9).

The witnesses agree these towns are named in turn — "Janum, and Bethtappuah, and Aphekah": "Beth-tappuah" means "the house of the apple" or fruit-tree, a token of the fruitfulness of the land Judah inherited; the very names of the cities often spoke of the goodness of the country, its fruit and springs and pleasant places; it teaches that God gave his people a good and fruitful land, the heritage rich in the bounty of his hand; the believer's portion in him likewise is fruitful and abundant, "a land of wheat, and barley, and vines, and fig trees... a land wherein thou shalt eat bread without scarceness" (Deut. 8:8-9), Beth-tappuah the house of fruit among Judah's cities.

So the witnesses make the verse Beth-tappuah the house of fruit: The witnesses note the Janum: a hill town. The witnesses note the Beth-tappuah: the house of the apple. The witnesses note the Aphekah: among the cities. The witnesses note the a fruitful land: a land wherein thou shalt eat bread without scarceness (Deut. 8:8-9). The expositors observe the Janum: a hill town. The expositors observe the Beth-tappuah: the house of the apple. The expositors observe the Aphekah: among the cities. The expositors observe the a fruitful land: a land wherein thou shalt eat bread without scarceness (Deut. 8:8-9). The believer is taught to mark the Janum, the Beth-tappuah, the Aphekah, the a fruitful land, and to rest in the sureness of his inheritance. The verse thus records that Beth-tappuah, "the house of fruit," is named among Judah's hill towns — a token of the fruitfulness of the land God gave, the heritage rich in his bounty, a land wherein thou shalt eat bread without scarceness (Deut. 8:8-9).
54:
"And Humtah, and Kirjatharba, which is Hebron, and Zior; nine cities with their villages:" The witnesses note Hebron, the city of the patriarchs and Caleb, among the hill cities. The witnesses note that Gill notes of Humtah we nowhere else read. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch note Kirjath-arba, or Hebron (see Joshua 10:3). The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Humtah — little known; the Kirjath-arba — which is Hebron; the the patriarchs' burial — and Caleb's portion; the covenant through ages — an everlasting covenant (Gen. 17:7).

The witnesses agree the group is crowned by Hebron, once Kirjath-arba, the most storied of Judah's cities: there Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob were buried in the cave of Machpelah, and there Caleb received his inheritance for wholly following the LORD (Josh. 14:14); the city rich in the memory of the fathers and of faithful Caleb stands among the tribe's towns; Hebron, later David's first royal seat, joins the patriarchs to the kingdom; it teaches that God's dealings with his people run on from age to age, the city of the fathers becoming the city of the king, the heritage bound up with his covenant faithfulness through generations, "I will establish my covenant... for an everlasting covenant" (Gen. 17:7), Hebron among the nine cities.

So the witnesses make the verse Hebron of the fathers: The witnesses note the Humtah: little known. The witnesses note the Kirjath-arba: which is Hebron. The witnesses note the the patriarchs' burial: and Caleb's portion. The witnesses note the covenant through ages: an everlasting covenant (Gen. 17:7). The expositors observe the Humtah: little known. The expositors observe the Kirjath-arba: which is Hebron. The expositors observe the the patriarchs' burial: and Caleb's portion. The expositors observe the covenant through ages: an everlasting covenant (Gen. 17:7). The believer is taught to mark the Humtah, the Kirjath-arba, the the patriarchs' burial, the covenant through ages, and to rest in the sureness of his inheritance. The verse thus records that Hebron, city of the patriarchs and of Caleb, crowns the group of nine — God's dealings running from age to age, the city of the fathers become the city of the king, his covenant faithfulness through generations, an everlasting covenant (Gen. 17:7).
55:
"Maon, Carmel, and Ziph, and Juttah," The witnesses note Maon, Carmel, and Ziph — places of David's wilderness wanderings. The witnesses note that Gill notes Maon was the dwelling of Nabal the Carmelite, and gave name to a wilderness where David hid from Saul. The witnesses note that Carmel and Ziph were near. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Maon and Carmel — nabal's home; the Ziph — david's hiding-place; the strengthened in God — by Jonathan (1 Sam. 23:16); the the way to the kingdom — if we suffer, we shall reign (2 Tim. 2:12).

The witnesses agree these towns are rich in the history of David's flight from Saul: Maon and Ziph gave their names to wildernesses where David hid, and Carmel was the home of Nabal and Abigail (1 Sam. 25:2); in the wilderness of Ziph the men of the place betrayed David, yet there Jonathan came and "strengthened his hand in God" (1 Sam. 23:16); the cities of Judah's heritage were the scenes of the anointed king's suffering and refuge before his throne; it teaches that the path to the kingdom often lies through the wilderness, the Son of David too despised and hunted before his glory, "strengthened... in God" amid the wilds, the believer's portion bound up with his Lord's rejection, "if we suffer, we shall also reign with him" (2 Tim. 2:12), Maon and Ziph among Judah's cities.

So the witnesses make the verse David's wilderness: The witnesses note the Maon and Carmel: Nabal's home. The witnesses note the Ziph: David's hiding-place. The witnesses note the strengthened in God: by Jonathan (1 Sam. 23:16). The witnesses note the the way to the kingdom: if we suffer, we shall reign (2 Tim. 2:12). The expositors observe the Maon and Carmel: Nabal's home. The expositors observe the Ziph: David's hiding-place. The expositors observe the strengthened in God: by Jonathan (1 Sam. 23:16). The expositors observe the the way to the kingdom: if we suffer, we shall reign (2 Tim. 2:12). The believer is taught to mark the Maon and Carmel, the Ziph, the strengthened in God, the the way to the kingdom, and to rest in the sureness of his inheritance. The verse thus records that Maon, Carmel, and Ziph, scenes of David's wilderness flight, are named among Judah's cities — the path to the kingdom through the wilderness, the anointed hunted before his throne, if we suffer we shall also reign with him (2 Tim. 2:12).
56:
"And Jezreel, and Jokdeam, and Zanoah," The witnesses note Jezreel in Judah, distinct from the famous Jezreel of the northern kingdom. The witnesses note that Gill notes this Jezreel in Judah is different from that royal seat of the kings of Israel, from which the valley of Esdraelon had its name. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set Jezreel, Jokdeam, and Zanoah. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Jezreel — of Judah, not the north; the Jokdeam — among the towns; the Zanoah — of the hill country; the God sows his people — i will sow her unto me in the earth (Hos. 2:23).

The witnesses agree this "Jezreel" in Judah is to be distinguished from the more famous Jezreel of the northern kings, the scene of Ahab and Jezebel's sins: Gill notes the difference; from this Judahite Jezreel came Ahinoam, one of David's wives (1 Sam. 25:43); the name "Jezreel" means "God will sow," a word of hope and increase; it teaches that God sows his people in the land and makes them to grow, planting them where he wills, the heritage a field of his sowing, "I will sow her unto me in the earth" (Hos. 2:23), Jezreel — God will sow — among Judah's hill towns.

So the witnesses make the verse Jezreel, God will sow: The witnesses note the Jezreel: of Judah, not the north. The witnesses note the Jokdeam: among the towns. The witnesses note the Zanoah: of the hill country. The witnesses note the God sows his people: I will sow her unto me in the earth (Hos. 2:23). The expositors observe the Jezreel: of Judah, not the north. The expositors observe the Jokdeam: among the towns. The expositors observe the Zanoah: of the hill country. The expositors observe the God sows his people: I will sow her unto me in the earth (Hos. 2:23). The believer is taught to mark the Jezreel, the Jokdeam, the Zanoah, the God sows his people, and to rest in the sureness of his inheritance. The verse thus records that Jezreel of Judah — "God will sow" — is named among the hill towns — God sowing and increasing his people, planting them where he wills, I will sow her unto me in the earth (Hos. 2:23).
57:
"Cain, Gibeah, and Timnah; ten cities with their villages:" The witnesses note Cain, Gibeah, and Timnah, closing this group of ten cities. The witnesses note that Gill notes of Cain we nowhere else read; whether named in memory of Cain, the son of Adam, is uncertain. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set Cain, Gibeah, and Timnah, ten cities. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Cain — obscure; the Gibeah — of the hill country; the Timnah — of Judah and Tamar; the the line carried on — judas begat Phares of Thamar (Matt. 1:3).

The witnesses agree the group closes with Timnah, a place of note in later history — there Judah the patriarch met Tamar (Gen. 38:12), and there Samson would later seek a wife among the Philistines (Judg. 14:1); the city of Judah's heritage was bound up with both the failings of the fathers and the workings of God's providence even through them; it teaches that the LORD carries forward his purpose through the tangled history of his people, bringing his redemptive line even through Timnah and Tamar to David and to Christ, "Judas begat Phares and Zara of Thamar" (Matt. 1:3), Timnah among the ten cities of Judah.

So the witnesses make the verse Timnah of the fathers: The witnesses note the Cain: obscure. The witnesses note the Gibeah: of the hill country. The witnesses note the Timnah: of Judah and Tamar. The witnesses note the the line carried on: Judas begat Phares of Thamar (Matt. 1:3). The expositors observe the Cain: obscure. The expositors observe the Gibeah: of the hill country. The expositors observe the Timnah: of Judah and Tamar. The expositors observe the the line carried on: Judas begat Phares of Thamar (Matt. 1:3). The believer is taught to mark the Cain, the Gibeah, the Timnah, the the line carried on, and to rest in the sureness of his inheritance. The verse thus records that Timnah, bound up with Judah and Tamar, closes the group of ten — God carrying his redemptive purpose through the tangled history of his people, even to David and Christ, Judas begat Phares of Thamar (Matt. 1:3).
58:
"Halhul, Bethzur, and Gedor," The witnesses note Halhul, Beth-zur, and Gedor, beginning a fourth group of hill cities. The witnesses note that Gill notes here begins a fourth list of the cities in the mountains; Halhul Jerom calls Ehul, a village near Hebron. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set Halhul, Beth-zur, and Gedor. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Halhul — near Hebron; the Beth-zur — the house of the rock; the Gedor — of the fourth group; the the LORD the true rock — my rock, and my fortress, my high tower (Ps. 18:2).

The witnesses agree a fourth group begins with "Halhul, Bethzur, and Gedor": "Beth-zur" means "the house of the rock," a stronghold of the hill country, later fortified in the days of the Maccabees; the city of the rock among Judah's towns may put the believer in mind of the true Rock, the LORD his strength and fortress; the strongholds of the heritage were a defence to God's people, yet their truest stronghold was himself; it teaches that the LORD is the rock and fortress of his own, a surer defence than any walled city, "the LORD is my rock, and my fortress... my high tower" (Ps. 18:2), Beth-zur the house of the rock among Judah's cities.

So the witnesses make the verse Beth-zur the house of the rock: The witnesses note the Halhul: near Hebron. The witnesses note the Beth-zur: the house of the rock. The witnesses note the Gedor: of the fourth group. The witnesses note the the LORD the true rock: my rock, and my fortress, my high tower (Ps. 18:2). The expositors observe the Halhul: near Hebron. The expositors observe the Beth-zur: the house of the rock. The expositors observe the Gedor: of the fourth group. The expositors observe the the LORD the true rock: my rock, and my fortress, my high tower (Ps. 18:2). The believer is taught to mark the Halhul, the Beth-zur, the Gedor, the the LORD the true rock, and to rest in the sureness of his inheritance. The verse thus records that Beth-zur, "the house of the rock," begins a fourth group — the strongholds of the heritage a defence, yet the LORD himself the truest stronghold, my rock, and my fortress, my high tower (Ps. 18:2).
59:
"And Maarath, and Bethanoth, and Eltekon; six cities with their villages:" The witnesses note Maarath, Beth-anoth, and Eltekon, closing this group of six cities. The witnesses note that Gill notes of these cities we have no account elsewhere. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set Maarath, Beth-anoth, and Eltekon, six cities. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Maarath — obscure; the Beth-anoth — little known; the Eltekon — god establishes; the six cities — with their villages; the the gift established — the LORD shall establish thee (Deut. 28:9).

The witnesses agree these towns, "Maarath, and Bethanoth, and Eltekon," are now obscure, yet the name "Eltekon" means "God is straight" or "God establishes," and "Beth-anoth" recalls a place of answered prayer or affliction; though men can give "no account" of these cities, their very names breathe of God; it teaches that the smallest and least-known parts of God's gift still bear his mark and witness to his faithfulness, the obscure towns established by him who sets all things firm; the believer may rest that what God gives, however hidden, is established by his hand, "the LORD shall establish thee" (Deut. 28:9), the six cities established in Judah's heritage.

So the witnesses make the verse God establishes: The witnesses note the Maarath: obscure. The witnesses note the Beth-anoth: little known. The witnesses note the Eltekon: God establishes. The witnesses note the six cities: with their villages. The witnesses note the the gift established: the LORD shall establish thee (Deut. 28:9). The expositors observe the Maarath: obscure. The expositors observe the Beth-anoth: little known. The expositors observe the Eltekon: God establishes. The expositors observe the six cities: with their villages. The expositors observe the the gift established: the LORD shall establish thee (Deut. 28:9). The believer is taught to mark the Maarath, the Beth-anoth, the Eltekon, the six cities, the the gift established, and to rest in the sureness of his inheritance. The verse thus records that these obscure cities close the group of six — the least-known parts of God's gift still bearing his mark, what he gives established by his hand, the LORD shall establish thee (Deut. 28:9).
60:
"Kirjathbaal, which is Kirjathjearim, and Rabbah; two cities with their villages:" The witnesses note Kirjath-baal, which is Kirjath-jearim — the resting-place of the ark for many years. The witnesses note that Gill notes Kirjath-baal and its several names, of which he speaks elsewhere. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the sixth group of only two towns, Kirjath-jearim and Rabbah. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Kirjath-baal — named for Baal; the which is Kirjath-jearim — where the ark rested; the Rabbah — the two cities; the the idol displaced — no agreement of the temple with idols (2 Cor. 6:16).

The witnesses agree this town, "Kirjathbaal, which is Kirjathjearim," is of great note: there the ark of God rested some twenty years after its return from the Philistines, in the house of Abinadab, until David brought it up to Zion (1 Sam. 7:1-2); the city once named for Baal became the dwelling-place of the ark of the LORD, the seat of the idol turned to the seat of God's presence; it teaches that the LORD displaces the idols and sets his glory where they once stood, the place of false worship made the resting-place of his ark, "what agreement hath the temple of God with idols?" (2 Cor. 6:16) — yet grace cleansed Kirjath-baal to hold the ark, among the cities of Judah.

So the witnesses make the verse Kirjath-jearim of the ark: The witnesses note the Kirjath-baal: named for Baal. The witnesses note the which is Kirjath-jearim: where the ark rested. The witnesses note the Rabbah: the two cities. The witnesses note the the idol displaced: no agreement of the temple with idols (2 Cor. 6:16). The expositors observe the Kirjath-baal: named for Baal. The expositors observe the which is Kirjath-jearim: where the ark rested. The expositors observe the Rabbah: the two cities. The expositors observe the the idol displaced: no agreement of the temple with idols (2 Cor. 6:16). The believer is taught to mark the Kirjath-baal, the which is Kirjath-jearim, the Rabbah, the the idol displaced, and to rest in the sureness of his inheritance. The verse thus records that Kirjath-jearim, once Kirjath-baal, where the ark rested twenty years, is named among Judah's cities — the seat of the idol turned to the resting-place of God's presence, grace displacing the idol, no agreement of the temple with idols (2 Cor. 6:16).
61:
"In the wilderness, Betharabah, Middin, and Secacah," The witnesses note the cities of Judah in the wilderness — not desert, but inhabited with towns. The witnesses note that Gill notes the wilderness of Judea was not a desert and uninhabited, but had many cities and villages in it. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set Beth-arabah, Middin, and Secacah. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the in the wilderness — of Judea; the Beth-arabah — and Middin; the Secacah — inhabited, not waste; the the desert made glad — the wilderness shall be glad (Isa. 35:1).

The witnesses agree even the wilderness of Judah held its cities — "Betharabah, Middin, and Secacah": Gill notes the wilderness "was not a desert and uninhabited, but had many cities"; the heritage reached even into the wild and lonely places, and there too God set towns and people; it teaches that the LORD gives his people even the wilderness for their portion, making the desert places fruitful and inhabited, no part of his gift a waste; the believer's heritage in him reaches even to the barren and hard places, where God yet plants and provides, "the wilderness and the solitary place shall be glad for them" (Isa. 35:1), the wilderness cities of Judah.

So the witnesses make the verse the wilderness cities: The witnesses note the in the wilderness: of Judea. The witnesses note the Beth-arabah: and Middin. The witnesses note the Secacah: inhabited, not waste. The witnesses note the the desert made glad: the wilderness shall be glad (Isa. 35:1). The expositors observe the in the wilderness: of Judea. The expositors observe the Beth-arabah: and Middin. The expositors observe the Secacah: inhabited, not waste. The expositors observe the the desert made glad: the wilderness shall be glad (Isa. 35:1). The believer is taught to mark the in the wilderness, the Beth-arabah, the Secacah, the the desert made glad, and to rest in the sureness of his inheritance. The verse thus records that even the wilderness of Judah held its cities — God giving his people the wild places too, no part of his gift a waste, the wilderness and the solitary place made glad (Isa. 35:1).
62:
"And Nibshan, and the city of Salt, and Engedi; six cities with their villages." The witnesses note En-gedi, a refuge of David, closing the wilderness group of six cities. The witnesses note that Gill notes of Nibshan no mention is made elsewhere. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch note the city of Salt and En-gedi, six cities with their villages. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Nibshan — obscure; the the city of Salt — the desolate region; the En-gedi — david's refuge and spring; the refreshment in the wild — the valley of Baca made a well (Ps. 84:6-7).

The witnesses agree the wilderness group closes with En-gedi, a place of springs and refuge in the desert by the Dead Sea: there David hid from Saul among the rocks of the wild goats, and there spared Saul's life in the cave (1 Sam. 24:1-7); the oasis of En-gedi, with its waters amid the barren wilds, was a place of shelter and mercy; the "city of Salt" beside it recalls the desolation of the region; it teaches that God provides springs of refreshment even in the wilderness of his people's trials, a place of refuge in the hardest country, "they go from strength to strength... who passing through the valley of Baca make it a well" (Ps. 84:6-7), En-gedi the oasis among the wilderness cities of Judah.

So the witnesses make the verse En-gedi the oasis: The witnesses note the Nibshan: obscure. The witnesses note the the city of Salt: the desolate region. The witnesses note the En-gedi: David's refuge and spring. The witnesses note the refreshment in the wild: the valley of Baca made a well (Ps. 84:6-7). The expositors observe the Nibshan: obscure. The expositors observe the the city of Salt: the desolate region. The expositors observe the En-gedi: David's refuge and spring. The expositors observe the refreshment in the wild: the valley of Baca made a well (Ps. 84:6-7). The believer is taught to mark the Nibshan, the the city of Salt, the En-gedi, the refreshment in the wild, and to rest in the sureness of his inheritance. The verse thus records that En-gedi, the oasis and refuge of David, closes the wilderness group — God providing springs of refreshment even in the wilderness of trial, the valley of Baca made a well (Ps. 84:6-7).
63:
"As for the Jebusites the inhabitants of Jerusalem, the children of Judah could not drive them out: but the Jebusites dwell with the children of Judah at Jerusalem unto this day." The witnesses note the Jebusites of Jerusalem, whom Judah could not drive out, dwelling with them to that day. The witnesses note that Gill notes the Jebusites, from whom the city was called Jebus, dwelt with Judah at Jerusalem. The witnesses note that the children of Judah could not drive them out. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the Jebusites — held Jerusalem; the Judah could not drive them out — the unconquered remnant; the dwelt with Judah — unto this day; the David would take it — the city of the great King (Matt. 5:35).

The witnesses agree the chapter closes on a note of failure that yet looked to a greater future — "the children of Judah could not drive them out: but the Jebusites dwell... at Jerusalem unto this day": the stronghold of Jebus held out against Judah, a remnant of the unconquered land in the very place destined to be the city of God; the failure to take it then marks the incompleteness of the conquest, yet Jerusalem would at last be wrested from the Jebusites by David and made the city of the great King (2 Sam. 5:6-9); it teaches that what God's people fail to win in one age he accomplishes in another, the stronghold long unconquered becoming at last the seat of his throne and the type of the heavenly city, "Jerusalem... the city of the great King" (Matt. 5:35), the Jebusites yet dwelling there in Joshua's day.

So the witnesses make the verse the Jebusites of Jerusalem: The witnesses note the the Jebusites: held Jerusalem. The witnesses note the Judah could not drive them out: the unconquered remnant. The witnesses note the dwelt with Judah: unto this day. The witnesses note the David would take it: the city of the great King (Matt. 5:35). The expositors observe the the Jebusites: held Jerusalem. The expositors observe the Judah could not drive them out: the unconquered remnant. The expositors observe the dwelt with Judah: unto this day. The expositors observe the David would take it: the city of the great King (Matt. 5:35). The believer is taught to mark the the Jebusites, the Judah could not drive them out, the dwelt with Judah, the David would take it, and to rest in the sureness of his inheritance. The verse thus records that Judah could not drive out the Jebusites of Jerusalem — the incompleteness of the conquest, yet the stronghold long unconquered would become David's city and the seat of God's throne, Jerusalem, the city of the great King (Matt. 5:35).
